Ellie's updates on parliamentary and constituency work

Below you’ll find Ellie’s e- newsletters since she was elected as MP for North Herefordshire. If you’re not already signed up to receive these updates every few weeks, head over here. If you love what Ellie’s doing, please do share this page with friends and family members whom you think would also like to see all that Ellie’s been up to.  As well as the electronic newsletters, Ellie publishes a physical newsletter called Herefordshire Matters 3 times a year.  You can read these via the hyperlink Herefordshire Matters.

January 2026

Well, there’s certainly a lot going on in the world…

The first few weeks back after Christmas have inevitably been dominated by foreign affairs.  With one outrageous statement from Trump after another, I’ve been urging the UK government to show a bit of backbone and stand up to Trump’s bullying tactics.  It’s clear that pandering to this dangerous US president does nothing but enable and encourage him.

In the past three weeks in Parliament I’ve spoken on numerous foreign affairs issues, including Venezuela, Gaza, Ukraine, Iran, Greenland, and Trump’s so-called ‘Board of Peace’.

I’ve also spoken about a wide range of domestic policy issues, including farming and APR (Agricultural Property Relief), concessionary bus schemes, embodied carbon in buildings, SEND provision, water pollution and the Water White Paper, and fuel poverty and the Warm Homes Plan.

I challenged the PM at PMQs last week about action on water pollution – but he completely swerved my question and launched a ridiculous tirade in response, earning himself a ticking-off from the Speaker.


I’ve met with the Minister for Social Care about funding for Herefordshire Council, and with the Secretary of State for Trade and Industry about barriers to export trade.

I’ve met with both Care England and the National Care Forum about reform to social care.

And last week in the constituency I spent a whole day ‘shadowing’ Becky, a social care worker, to learn more about the vital work that she and so many like her do to support people to live in their own homes.  We visited half a dozen different people in the Ledbury area, and it was a real privilege to sit and chat with them and to hear from Becky and her colleagues about the joys and the challenges of working in the care sector. This is all part of my ‘Frontline Fridays’, when I spend a full day immersing myself in one of the frontline services that play such a vital role in our community.

Also at home in Herefordshire I met with the Principals of four local further education colleges (Hereford Sixth Form, the Tech college HLNSC, the Art college HCA, and the Royal National College for the Blind) about the impact of changes to BTECs and the introduction of V-levels; as a result, I’ve written to the Minister.  

I’ve been keeping in close touch with Herefordshire Council, and taking every opportunity to lobby Ministers to review the unfair outcome of the so-called ‘Fair Funding Review’ which simply doesn’t recognise the particular challenges in Herefordshire.  And I’m working with the local hospital to press for government to address issues with funding and discharges relating to patients who come over the border from Powys.

I also attended the official opening of the new Community Diagnostic Centre in Hereford (left or top photo of the two above), which is already providing great service to constituents; took part in an event on tax justice in Much Marcle; held a constituency surgery in Holmer; and had a wonderful time at our local Green Party’s annual ‘GreenFeast’ potluck lunch in Weobley (some fellow attendees right or bottom photo of the two above).  I’ve also enjoyed various local arts-related activities: a fantastic exhibition at the Green Dragon showcasing blacksmithing work by young people participating in activities at the Cart Shed; the light installation ‘Time’ at Hereford Cathedral; a Flicks in the Sticks film showing in Eye, near Leominster; and the movie Hamnet, which of course was filmed partly in Weobley.  I also had a wonderful time volunteering at the Queenswood Parkrun on Saturday!

In London, I spoke to 50 journalism Masters students; spoke at an online event with UCL’s Constitution Unit on how Parliament deals with smaller parties; took part in the RSPB’s Big Garden Birdwatch in Lambeth Palace Gardens; and met with dozens of members of the Association of Green Councillors who were visiting Parliament.

I also attended a deeply moving Holocaust Memorial Day ceremony in Parliament, hearing first-hand testimony from a concentration camp survivor.  We must never, ever forget.

Three new people joined my team in early January, and we had a super-productive team strategy meeting in Leominster, bringing together my Westminster and Constituency teams to plan for the coming months.

And media-wise I’ve appeared on Politics Live, BBC Hereford and Worcester, and Sunshine Radio as well as in many newspapers.

This week, the first thing I did when I reached Parliament was to attend an event on health and wellbeing in farming, organised by the All-Party Group on Farming (of which I am an Officer).  I then spoke at Department for Work and Pensions (DWP) questions to press Ministers for much swifter action in response to complaints – one of my constituents has been waiting 15 weeks for a response, when the department’s target time is supposedly 15 days.  This is just one of many examples where me and my team of caseworkers take up issues on behalf of constituents. Tomorrow I’m meeting with the Care Quality Commission and with the Police and Crime Commissioner; this week I’ll be trying to ask questions of the Treasury and of the Department of Business and Trade; and on Thursday we have a debate on ‘Protecting and Restoring River Habitats’, where of course I will be speaking!

Last week I coordinated a cross-party letter from River Wye catchment MPs to the Minister for Water, asking for a meeting regarding the pollution that is still blighting the Wye and blocking building in North Herefordshire.  My lobbying last year managed to persuade the Minister to force the Environment Agency to publish the Diffuse Water Pollution Plan (DWPP) – but, shockingly, that Plan doesn’t even assess the option of a Water Protection Zone.  So while it shows that 100% implementation of all existing measures simply wouldn’t solve the problems, the government still has no actual plan to clean up the Wye and its tributaries – especially the Lugg, which is worst hit of all our local rivers.  So, this is a key focus for me at the moment, and I’ll be doing everything I possibly can to press for the long-overdue action our local rivers need.

As always, if you’re a constituent and there’s anything you’d like to raise with me, please do email!  A reminder that I host regular surgeries too, if your issue would be best supported by a confidential, face to face meeting.  The next date is 6th February in Ledbury.

With best wishes,

Ellie

You received this email because you signed up to Ellie’s mailing list on her website, took a campaign action, or gave us your address at an event. Published and promoted by Dr Ellie Chowns (Green Party), c/o 9 The Buttercross, Leominster, HR6 8BN.

If you are forwarding this email to a friend, then please mention they can sign up themselves for Ellie's updates here.

October 2nd 2025

Hello again – here’s a quick update on what I’ve been up to in recent weeks!

As you may be aware, Parliament has a rather odd timetable in early autumn – we start up at Westminster at the beginning of September, same as the school term – but then we have ‘conference recess’ for 3 weeks in late September / early October, due to all of the annual party conferences.  It’s all a bit odd really – I wouldn’t organise things this way if I had the choice – but heigh ho, it seems to be traditional!  The Green Party’s conference is in Bournemouth this year in early October, so I am looking forward to a bracing sea swim or two! 😊

But for now, let me give you a quick summary of the last 10 days in Parliament and the first half of conference recess.

On Monday 8th September I took part in the first meeting of the cross-party talks of the Casey Commission on Social Care, together with representatives of the other main parties.  I’m glad these talks have finally commenced, but I have to say I’m impatient for more rapid progress.  I’m also hoping that the Casey Commission might visit North Herefordshire to hear from local people and organisations with experience of social care – I’ll keep you posted if we’re able to arrange this.

On 9th September I was really delighted and honoured to win the ‘Rivers and Seas’ award at the Political Purpose Awards, recognising my work in Parliament to tackle water pollution.  However there is still so much more to be done…

Also that week I appeared on ITV and on Sky News discussing topics including migration and the visit to the UK of Israeli President Herzog.  I continue to speak out to challenge the UK government to do far more to stop the horror in Gaza.

Back in the constituency I attended the Hereford College of Arts graduation ceremony.

That evening, I attended a lovely event at St Michael’s Hospice celebrating the 40th anniversary of the Hereford-Muheza Link Society, which organises exchanges between Hereford medical staff and their counterparts in Tanzania.

I also have been busy making grants from my Constituency Fund to a wide range of brilliant local organisations, including The Cart Shed, ECHO, Vennture, Home-Start and Close House.

Back in Westminster the following Monday, I took part in a parliamentary debate on Special Educational Needs and Disabilities (SEND), prompted by a public petition.  When a petition on the official petitions website gets over 100,000 signatures, it triggers a debate in Westminster Hall (the second chamber of the House of Commons).  This debate was literally standing room only, with dozens of MPs waiting to speak – I have never seen it so busy in that chamber!  There was a slight problem because MPs have to be in a seat in order to be called to speak – so the doorkeepers were doing quite a lot of ‘herding’ to try to ensure that everyone got a turn.  I’ve been contacted by lots of parents and teachers raising their concerns about the SEND system so I am doing all I can to represent them and ensure their voices are heard.

On the same day, my select committee (the Environmental Audit Committee) was meeting to finalise our report on Flood Resilience, which I hope will help push the government to invest more in this crucial area – I know it’s a topic close to many of our hearts.

Later on we had to vote on something like 12 votes on the Employment Rights Bill – which took about 3 hours in total of walking round through the division lobbies.  I think I have explained previously in this newsletter just how ridiculous the voting system is – an unbelievable waste of everyone’s time when it could just as easily be done electronically, meaning each vote would take 15 seconds instead of 15 minutes.  Roll on modernisation!  I’m continuing to press for this every chance I get…

Finally at the end of a long day (about 11pm) we had a debate on council housing, where I pressed the Housing Minister once more to set a target for social housing. Yet again he failed to explain why he continues to refuse to do this, but I will keep up the pressure as it’s an absolute scandal that 170,000 children in the UK are homeless.

Later that week, after the House rose for recess, I took part in a really energising Green Party awayday, planning for the 2029 general election – I know it seems early, but it’s good to be prepared and we have ambitious plans! 😊

Back in the constituency, I have been making the most of the opportunity to visit as many local organisations as possible during the conference recess.

First up, I visited Bromyard Food Bank & Money Advice Centre to meet with some of the energy advisors from Severn Wye Energy Agency, who provide really valuable support and advice for people struggling with high energy bills and the cost of living.

Then I headed to my Leominster constituency office for a catch-up with various members of my team of caseworkers about the many cases people seek our help with.  The team have developed a huge amount of expertise and it’s truly heart-warming to read the emails from constituents who have had their problems solved with assistance from our office – one of the highlights of the job!

In the evening I spent a moving couple of hours with Hereford Transgender Support Group. They shared with me their concerns about the impact of the recent Supreme Court ruling, urged me to represent their views in Parliament, and explained just how valuable the support group is.

 

The following week I visited the Centre for Advanced Timber Technology at NMITE to meet their new cohort of Sustainable Construction degree students, as well as observing a professional development course that had attracted dozens of participants from all over the country.

I spent time with Herefordshire Kinship Carers Group, hearing about their experiences of caring for their grandchildren.  Kinship care is so important where it’s possible, and such carers need all the support we can give them.

I visited Hereford Medical Group surgery – by far the biggest surgery in Herefordshire.  Although it’s in Hereford city, it serves quite a lot of my constituents, and so I wanted to discuss the issues they’d raised with me about access to services.

Next I visited West Mercia Women’s Aid to discuss their work supporting victims and survivors of domestic abuse, and their concerns about the family court system works.  On this, as on all the other issues, I’ll be following up through Parliamentary Questions, letters to Ministers, or whatever other mechanism is most appropriate.

Forestry England invited me to visit Haugh Woods near Mordiford to see their work on the ground protecting nature, providing public access to woodland, and sustainably harvesting timber from the woods.

On Wednesday evening I held one of my regular Public Meetings in Bromyard, at the QE Secondary School.  I hold these meetings roughly once every three months, in different venues around the county, to allow any of my constituents to come along and ask me any question they like.  We had good discussions about all sorts of issues including river pollution, road repairs, and more.

My next public meeting will be on Friday 28th November at Lady Hawkins School in Kington.  Doors open at 6.15 for refreshments and the Q&A starts at 7.00pm, running to 8.30pm.  You can ask me anything about my parliamentary and constituency work, either via a roving microphone, or by submitting a written question on the night if that is what you prefer.  I hope to see you there!

While I was at the QE School I was able to visit the lovely new sports facilities they’ve recently opened – a 3G pitch for football, and a revamped netball court.  Having spent many years of my life standing by the side of Herefordshire football pitches while my sons played, it all felt very familiar!  The new facilities are great – enabling more kids to play, year-round. I’ve seen for myself just how valuable sport is for young people.

The visits continued… next day I was at Herefordshire and Worcestershire Group Training Association, meeting their new intake of engineering apprentices and admiring some of their new facilities, including very fancy 3D printers!

Then I met with Landau, a local charity that provides employment support to help people get back into work.   It was great to hear from one of the people they’ve helped just how much of a difference it made.

In the afternoon I was in Kington for one of my regular constituency surgeries – I try to get to all corners of the constituency! The next surgeries coming up are in Ledbury on the afternoon of 17th October and Leominster in the morning of 8th November.  These surgeries are for one-to-one face to face conversations with constituents on matters which are affecting you personally and where you may need help.

After my surgery I headed down Kington High Street to pop into all the shops, have a chat with the owners, and drop off a card so that they have my contact details in case they ever want to get in touch about any issues.  It was really good to hear so many of the shopkeepers report that business has been good recently!

And on Friday I rounded off the week with a few more interesting outings…

I hopped on to the newly-restarted bus between Leominster and Kington (hooray!) run by local bus company Sargeants.  I know many local people have been really keen for this service to be reinstated so it was great to be able to celebrate this – and what a beautiful bus ride it is too!  You can find the timetable online here.

In the afternoon I headed to the east of the county, right on the border on the Malvern Hills, to officially open the newly-resurfaced footpath and handrail on the path up to British Camp. I absolutely love the Malvern Hills and have climbed that path many times in all weathers – including in icy snow one New Year when the handrail was definitely very much needed on the descent! 😊

Finally I spent a couple of hours on the Friday evening with the Colwall Youth Project, chatting with young people, staff and volunteers.  It was great fun – helping out with cooking the evening’s food (stir fry – delicious!) and playing a highly competitive game of pool which I lost in a nail-biting finish!

Honestly, I love the fact that this job is so varied – I get to visit all these really wonderful projects, people and organisations doing so much across the county to build and strengthen our communities and provide support to people to need it.  There’s so much to celebrate and pay tribute to!

I’ve taken a few days’ holiday at the start of this week and will then be at the aforementioned Green Party conference in Bournemouth.  Meanwhile I’ll be cracking on, together with my team, with responding to all the emails and requests for help that we get.  Please don’t hesitate to get in touch if there’s anything you’d like to raise with me!

Thanks and all the very best,

Ellie

You received this email because you signed up to Ellie’s mailing list on her website, took a campaign action, or gave us your address at an event. Published and promoted by Helen Heathfield on behalf of Ellie Chowns, c/o 9 The Buttercross, Leominster, HR6 8BN.

If you are forwarding this email to a friend, then please mention they can sign up themselves for Ellie's updates here.

September 8th 2025

Hello again!  It’s a new Parliamentary ‘term’ and I’m resolved again to send regular updates, written as I head up to Westminster on the Monday morning train!

Parliament re-started last Monday, 1st September. I’ll give you a run-down of the past week in Westminster shortly, but first, an update on what I’ve been doing at home in the constituency.

In the Constituency

On Friday I held my regular constituency surgery, this time in Wellington village.  Then, after a meeting with Onside Advocacy, which provides mental health support in Herefordshire, I headed north to Staunton-on-Arrow to visit some Ice Age Ponds with local wildlife experts, farmers, and representatives of the Freshwater Habitats Trust.  Herefordshire is a nationally-important area for these ponds, thanks to quirks of geology.  I managed to get covered in mud (!) but luckily my next visit, to the Rodd and the Sidney Nolan Trust, gave me an opportunity to get cleaned up 😊.  The Rodd is where Sidney worked for many years and is now an arts centre, open to visitors and working with local schools to provide opportunities for children and young people to explore their creativity.  I loved the outdoor Earth Photo exhibition!

Next up I headed to Leintwardine to visit the multi-award-winning Fiddlers Elbow fish & chip shop, owned and run by friendly couple Linzi and Dom.  We had a good chat about the joys and challenges of running a small business, I got a behind-the-scenes look at the gleaming kitchen, and then of course I had to get my Friday night supper there – delicious and highly recommended! 😊

Collage from top right, clockwise, ending in centre:

Fiddlers Elbow fish and chip shop; Leominster Food Fayre; Herefordshire Wildlife Trust Moth Survey; Ice Age Pond visit with Herefordshire Pond Group; H Art preview in Leintwardine; Sidney Nolan Trust; with the Leominster Fish Taster.

From there it was a short walk to Leintwardine Church for the opening night of their H.Art exhibition, with nearly two dozen artists and makers plus live music making for a wonderfully creative and festive atmosphere.  H.Art is a really brilliant festival of local creativity with a huge range of arts and crafts on show across the county – check out the wonderful programme here – there is so much to explore!

On Saturday, Herefordshire Wildlife Trust was holding a series of river-themed events; I went along to a fascinating Moth Survey in Marden, where the very knowledgeable Robin introduced us to dozens of varieties of beautiful moths, caught overnight in a humane moth trap (basically, egg boxes and a light) and released in the morning.  I hadn’t realised that there are so many moth species in the UK - about 2500 moth species, compared to just 59 butterfly species.

After popping in to say hello to the wonderful team running Leominster Repair Café, I went to explore the Leominster Food Fayre. It was great to see the town buzzing with stallholders and shoppers making the most of all the amazing local produce we have to offer, including of course apple juice and cider, and honey and cheese, and all sorts of other delights!  And I enjoyed getting a photo with a 10-foot tall baker (on stilts)!

Finally on Saturday afternoon I took part in a local Green Party action day in the town, knocking on doors and chatting to people, checking that they know how to contact me, and asking for feedback on my regular newsletters – really lovely conversations.

In Westminster

So what about Parliament?  Well, it’s been a busy week in politics. Shortly after MPs reconvened last Monday we had an oral statement from the Foreign Secretary about the situation in the Middle East, and I challenged him yet again to do more.  There are many levers the UK government could use to pressure the Israeli government to stop the war crimes; I continue to push the government to fulfil its obligations under international law to do everything possible to prevent genocide.

Tuesday was results day in the Green Party’s internal leadership election, in which I had been standing alongside co-leader Adrian Ramsay.  Zack Polanski won with a resounding majority; I wish him all the very best and will of course work closely and constructively with him!

On Wednesday I challenged the Prime Minister at PMQs to finally stop the Rosebank oil project, which is totally incompatible with our climate obligations. The PM gave an extremely disappointing answer, completely avoiding the question.  The pantomime of PMQs is deeply frustrating to anyone who cares about genuine debate and scrutiny of government.

On Thursday at DEFRA questions I invited the Farming and Water ministers to come to North Herefordshire to visit the Wyescapes Landscape Recovery Project, which I visited recently.  Wyescapes brings together 49 farms in a farmer-led programme which protects soils, restores wildlife, tackles pollution and sustains food production.  As it turns out, with Labour’s ministerial changes over the weekend, we now have a new Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs, and a new Farming Minister, so I will be writing to them this week to highlight the challenges we face in Herefordshire and to ask for their support.

Also on Thursday I highlighted the concerns of huge numbers of people about the proscription of Palestine Action under terrorism legislation, and asked for Parliament to revisit this decision with more information from government.

And finally last week, I was honoured to be chosen unanimously by the Green MPs as the Leader of the Green Group in Parliament.  The role involves liaising with Parliamentary authorities, and leading for the Greens during set-piece events in Parliament such as PMQs and the King’s Speech.  I will of course be working very closely with Zack, Rachel and Mothin, our dynamic new Green Party leadership team, as well as with my excellent MP colleagues.

Coming up this week we have debates on the Renters Rights Bill, on Chagos, on the Bus Services Bill, and much more. However, the main focus for me personally is the first meeting of the cross-party talks on social care as part of the work of the Casey Commission. Social care has been a ‘Cinderella issue’ for far too many years. It’s vital that parties work together to find common ground so that we can end the scandal of too many people not getting the care they need, and the huge stress and unfairness of care being both a postcode lottery and a life lottery - with some facing huge costs, while others are lucky to escape with none.  The Green Party believes that we should pool the risk of needing social care, in the same way that the NHS pools our risk of needing medical care.

So on that note, I’ll return to reading more of the numerous already-existing reports on social care, and will report back to you all again next week!

Just a couple of final things before I sign off:

  1. Could you help deliver my leaflets in North Herefordshire for about an hour, once every 4 months? If so please let me know here – I’ll be hugely grateful – it’s so important to keep all constituents informed about what I’m doing and it’s a huge team effort to do so!  Thank you.
  2. Have you ever thought about standing for election?  I’m continuing to run ‘New Voices’ events, both in person and online, for anyone who’d like to find out more about what it really involves.  The events can useful for those at the very start of their considerations, or those who feel ready to take next steps.  Find out more and sign up here!
  3. Do you know a community project or charity in North Herefordshire that could benefit from my Constituency Fund?  I give grants of up to £1000 to projects that tackle inequality through care and creativity.  More info here.

OK, that’s all for now.  Thanks and all the best!

Ellie

If you are forwarding this email to a friend, then please mention they can sign up themselves for Ellie's updates here.

May 19th 2025

Hello all.  I’m writing this on the train to London on Monday morning, and back in the rhythm I hope of a brief weekly update!

In Westminster

Last week was a bit different from normal as I spent three full days in Bill Committee for the Planning and Infrastructure Bill.  As I’ve mentioned before, the Bill Committee spends many days on line by line scrutiny of the bill, going through every clause and multiple amendments and proposed new clauses (hundreds in this case).

Without getting into the weeds too much, the Planning and Infrastructure Bill (PIB) is a bit of a mixed bag – some good bits that will help fix issues in the planning system, and some deeply worrying ones that risk seriously undermining nature protection.  I’ve been working closely with a wide range of concerned organisations such as the Wildlife Trusts to table amendments to try to fix the main problems with the Bill.

In particular I am concerned that the Bill doesn’t protect irreplaceable habitats, that it’s ‘overall improvement test’ is too weak, and that it sweeps away what’s called the ‘mitigation hierarchy’ i.e. the principle that one should seek first to avoid environmental damage, then minimise it, and only as a last resort offset it.  The Office for Environmental Protection (OEP) – the government’s own watchdog – has raised serious concerns about this too.  It’s really important to have a Green voice in the room, voicing the concerns of all those concerned about nature protection, and putting the government Minister on the spot about why he’s ignoring the advice of his own independent advisers.  The Bill says on the front page that the Secretary of State confirms it won’t lead to reduced environmental protection – but sadly that’s clearly not true.  So I’m doing everything possible to pressure the government to recognise the truth of the OEP’s concerns and to take their advice to amend the Bill.

Of course, the way these things work in committee is that the government will never say ‘oh yes, OK, you’re right, we’ll vote for your amendment’ – but the aim is to ratchet up the pressure so that the government goes away and thinks about it and comes back at the next stage (Report Stage) with an amendment that has the same effect, but where they can claim the credit! 😊 Politics, eh… 

If they don’t accept these amendments at Report Stage then the campaign continues in the Lords, and it’s possible that the Lords (which is less partisan) will be able to persuade the government to make the changes… so it’s a long old process but again, that’s politics – chip chip chipping away to improve things (and to stop things getting worse…).

Anyway, so after three days on the trot in Bill Committee last week (quite a slog, and we continue this coming week), I was also in Westminster on Friday for the first day of a 2-day Report Stage for the Terminally Ill Adults (End of Life) Bill, commonly known as the Assisted Dying Bill.  The Chamber was again full for the whole 5 hours of debate, but sadly the tone of the debate was not quite as measured and considered as for the Second Reading debate in November.  At Report Stage, the whole Chamber considers the Bill as it has been amended in Bill Committee stage; and also considers additional / further amendments.  For the Assisted Dying Bill, it had a hugely long Committee Stage (90 hours in total over more than 2 months; much more than any of the government Bills I’ve been involved with); I believe there were several hundred amendments tabled, of which about 150 have been accepted in Committee.  Now, at Report Stage, there are dozens more amendments to consider.  Some are drafting amendments – small tweaks to language to clarify things legally – while others are major changes.  If an amendment proposes a change so significant that it would make the bill unworkable, it’s called a ‘wrecking amendment’.

There are so many amendments to this Bill to consider that the Speaker grouped them into two groups, to be discussed on two different days.  Even so, the debate on Friday did not have time for all of the amendments to be individually proposed; and given that every vote takes 15 minutes, there’s certainly no time to vote on them all.  This is actually quite normal at Report Stage – generally speaking only a small number of amendments are voted on at Report.

From my perspective, as a supporter of the Bill (though recognising the concerns and principles of those who oppose it), some of the rhetoric employed by opponents of the bill on this occasion was a little disappointing.  Emotions are clearly running high.  Nevertheless, it was a useful and generally constructive debate; and I’m glad we have another day to consider the second group of amendments. And indeed many of the issues being brought as amendments at Report Stage have already been discussed in great detail at Committee stage, of course.

It's a huge privilege and responsibility to be part of the process for legislation as significant as this, and is something I take incredibly seriously.  I read every letter and email sent to me on the topic, and am often very moved indeed by the personal stories people share with me.  On this legislation, more than any other, my vote counts – because this is a conscience vote, so the other parties are not whipped, so there is no inbuilt majority for any one party.  On this issue, I can truly say I believe every single MP is thinking very deeply about the issues.

Back to the Constituency

On Friday afternoon I took the train home and went straight to Bromyard Theatre to watch the film ‘Conclave’ which I have been hoping to see for months – a fascinating and very topical story.  I recommend the book even more than the film!

On Saturday morning I held the second of my ‘New Voices’ events, designed to encourage new people to get actively involved in politics. 

We were a group of half a dozen of varying ages, and it was really great to meet everyone and have such a wide-ranging discussion of politics, values, motivations etc.  I’m really passionate about the potential for politics to make a positive difference (despite all the frustrations!) and so this is one way I feel I can reach out to encourage others to step up and make their voices heard.  I’ll keep on running these events, so do let me know if you’d like to come along.

Next I had the joyful experience of helping to judge the ‘Herefordshire Young Farmers does Mamma Mia’ competition at the HYF Rally.  It was brilliant!  This is the second time I have been asked to judge a talent contest – something I never realised was part of the MP job description 😊 – and it’s always such a joy! 

The 9 teams all did brilliantly with some really impressively synchronised dance routines.  A particular highlight: two teams rewrote some of the lyrics to Abba songs to fit the farming theme, so we had ‘Super Tractor’ and my personal favourite ‘MooMoo Mia’, complete with a dancer in an inflatable cow suit!  Huge thanks to Max for being my co-judge and huge congratulations to all involved.

More serious business after that; back to Ledbury to meet Jonathan the landlord of the Prince of Wales (PoW) pub to discuss business rates, employer NICs and the various other taxes and policies that small businesses contend with.

The PoW is a great example of a proper independent local pub with a great reputation for a wide variety of independent and local beers and ciders – and music to boot!  Small businesses face particular challenges – especially with the changes to employer NICs, which as Greens we have opposed – we think government should raise funds for public services by taxing wealth, not work.

Also at the weekend I did a couple of interviews, for BBC Breakfast TV and for LBC radio, on the UK-EU reset summit happening today (Monday).  I am certain that our best future lies in the closest possible relationship with the EU – and I know the majority of Brits agree; every opinion poll for the last 2 or 3 years show that most Brits think we’d be better off in the EU.  I very much hope the reset today is the start of a proper rebuilding of that relationship.

Looking ahead

In addition to the UK-EU summit, today Parliament is debating the Mental Health Bill, and I’ve got an Environmental Audit Committee hearing on flood resilience – at which my constituent ‘Flood Mary’ (Mary Long-Dhonau, who’s an indefatigable campaigner on property flood resilience) will be giving evidence.  Tomorrow and Thursday I’m back in the Planning and Infrastructure Bill committee; and on Wednesday I’m meeting disabled constituents and campaigners who are deeply concerned (as I am) about welfare cuts for disabled people.  I’ll also be on Matt Chorley’s show on 5 Live in the afternoon, so do tune in!

The following week, Parliament is in recess, so I’ll be out and about in the constituency doing as many visits as possible.

I’ll end with a quick reminder about upcoming events; a funding opportunity and a request for your feedback.

On Thursday 29th May, I have organised a drop in with West Midlands Railways (WMR) at the Constituency Office, 9 The Buttercross, Leominster HR6 8BN.  If you’d like to meet senior representatives from WMR to hear about their plans and to feedback or comment on their service, do come along between 1.00 and 2.30pm.  There’s no need to book.

On Friday 6th June I’m holding another Public Meeting / Q&A on my work at Leominster Sports and Social Club from 7.00 – 8.30pm – similar to the one I did in Ledbury in February. Doors open at 6.15pm for tea & biscuits beforehand.  Please do come along and ask me questions about my work as an MP or matters of national policy and how they affect you.  You can either submit a written question, or ask a question yourself on the night.

Do you know of any local projects that need a bit of support?  I’ve set up a Constituency Fund with money from my MP salary to support projects in the constituency that make life better by tackling inequality through care and creativity.  I’ve already made some donations, and I’d like to hear about any projects that might be suitable for some extra funding.  You can find out more about how to apply, and see examples of projects already funded, at this link. 

Do let me know if there’s anything in particular you’d like to hear about in my email updates, or would like me to cover in my TikTok explainers.

And (assuming you’re a constituent) please feel free as always to email me about any issue that concerns you – and do please share this update with anyone you think might be interested!

With thanks and best wishes,

Ellie

You received this email because you signed up to Ellie’s mailing list on her website, took a campaign action, or gave us your address at an event. Published and promoted by Helen Heathfield on behalf of Ellie Chowns (Green Party), c/o 9 The Buttercross, Leominster, HR6 8BN.

If you are forwarding this email to a friend, then please mention they can sign up themselves for Ellie's updates here.

Mid May 2025

Hello everyone! 

There’s been a bit of a break between my last update and this one, so there’s huge amounts to update you on 😊.  However I don’t think I can really manage to summarise the entire last month, so I’ve just given you a detailed update on last week, a few highlights from the previous month. And I’ve tried to make it easier for you to skim-read, by using bold text to highlight key things – I hope this is helpful!

Last week

Last Tuesday I spoke in the Chamber on social care – pressing the govt once more to start the long-promised cross-party talks – I think this is about the 4th or 5th time I’ve had to bring this up.  Wes Streeting promised these talks in January, cancelled them in February, the day before they were due to start, and is now suggesting that he’s handed the responsibility over to Baroness Casey.  But her review has hardly started; and in any case the Secretary of State shouldn’t be outsourcing political leadership – which is the point I made in the House last week.  I will keep on pressing for the government to grasp the nettle on social care which is such a huge issue that desperately needs concerted political attention and cooperation.

I also spoke in the VE Day debate last week, where I told the story of my grandfather. He was a navigator on a Stirling Bomber shot down over France in August 1943.  Only 2 of the crew of 7 survived.  He was hidden by incredibly brave Resistance families who risked their lives to save his, the life of a stranger.  After 4 months he managed to escape over the Pyrenees and back to the UK.  I had the honour last year of meeting Monique and Jean, children of the couple who hid my grandfather in their bakery.  May the bravery of all who resisted fascism inspire us all today.

On Wednesday morning I appeared on BBC 5 Live’s MP panel, responding to questions from callers.  Later that day, I attended the first part of the debate on PiP reforms last Wednesday.  Many MPs are horrified, as I am, by the government’s attempts to balance the books on the backs of those who are sick and disabled.  Instead, the Greens have long been calling for a wealth tax to fund the public services we all rely on.

Photo: Nature Friendly Farming Network farm visit hosted by Broadward Hall Farm.

I tackled the Minister for Farming (yet again) in DEFRA questions on Thursday, pressing him to significantly improve support for nature-friendly farming.  The previous weekend I had visited two farms in North Herefordshire and met a number of farmers, all of whom are deeply frustrated with the government’s failure to support the sector.  There’s been a real collapse of trust – such a crying shame, when farming is essential not just for food security but also for climate action and nature protection.

Later that day I attended the VE Day 2 minute silence in Parliament Square, which was very moving. 

I then took the train home and went straight to the Bishops Frome VE Day commemorations, involving the traditional (for the village) burning of a beacon in the shape of a Straw Man – very impressive!

On Friday and Saturday I was as usual busy in the constituency.   I visited a wonderful rewilding project which was bursting with wildlife!  

Then on to TPG DisableAids, a Hereford-based family business that provides all sorts of disability equipment and mobility aids.  After a stop off at the independent family-owned pub the Bell Inn at Tillington for a delicious home-cooked lunch (celebrating Independent Hospitality Week), I held my constituency surgery in Wellington Community Centre. 

As it happens I was back in Wellington the following day for a ‘Water in Wellington’ history walk – part of the Herefordshire Histories Festival.  It was really fascinating – I hadn’t realised that the village only got mains water in the mid-1970s!

 

After that it was straight to Hereford Cathedral for the very first graduation ceremony for NMITE Herefordshire’s new engineering-focused university.  It was a wonderful celebration – tribute to all those who have worked so hard to set up this pioneering institution, and to the hard work of all the graduating students.

 

Photo of Water in Wellington participants.

Previous weeks

I think rather than providing a blow-by-blow account of the previous weeks, I’ll just encourage you to check out my social media feeds if you want to catch up on what else I’ve been doing 😊.  I’m still trying to do weekly video updates with a one-minute rundown of the main highlights.  And I have joined TikTok!  I want to help make Parliament more transparent and accessible, so I am trying to do quick informal video ‘explainers’ of different bits of Parliament’s internal workings.  Check it out and do let me know if there’s something in particular you’d like me to (try to) explain!

I do also want to pay HUGE tribute to everyone in the Green Party across the country who worked so hard to secure brilliant results for the Greens in the local elections on 1st May!  Yet again we increased our number of councillors (for the 8th year running!) – and the West Midlands in particular did brilliantly!

Above: Ellie on local election day, in Worcester.

Coming up soon:

This week in Parliament my key focus is the Planning and Infrastructure Bill – we’ve got three full days in Bill Committee, and I am tabling lots of amendments and speaking on many others.  Like many nature organisations, I’m worried that the Bill’s provisions will lead to erosion of protections for nature.  I very much hope the government will heed our calls to make changes to the bill to ensure that irreplaceable habitats are protected and that development doesn’t come at the expense of nature protection.

On Friday this week Parliament is sitting for the Report Stage of the Terminally Ill Adults (End of Life) Bill – commonly known as the Assisted Dying Bill.  This Bill has been through Second Reading (last November) and Committee Stage (for several months) and is now returning to the main chamber of the Commons where MPs will consider more amendments.  I very much hope that the debate will be similar in tone to the November debate, which I think showed Parliament at its best: mutually respectful, thoughtful, and considered.

I’ll be back in the constituency at the weekend of course.  And I have a few things coming up to let you know about:

On Thursday 29th May, I have organised a drop in with West Midlands Railways (WMR) at the Constituency Office, 9 The Buttercross, Leominster HR6 8BN.  If you’d like to meet senior representatives from WMR to hear about their plans and to feedback or comment on their service, do come along between 1.00 and 2.30pm.  There’s no need to book.

On Friday 6th June I’m holding another Public Meeting / Q&A on my work at Leominster Sports and Social Club from 7.00 – 8.30pm – similar to the one I did in Ledbury in February. Doors open at 6.15pm for tea & biscuits beforehand.  Please do come along and ask me questions about my work as an MP or matters of national policy and how they affect you.  You can either submit a written question, or ask a question yourself on the night.

Finally: do you know of any local projects that need a bit of support?  I’ve set up a Constituency Fund with money from my MP salary to support projects in the constituency that make life better by tackling inequality through care and creativity.  I’ve already made some donations, and I’d like to hear about any projects that might be suitable for some extra funding.  You can find out more about how to apply, and see examples of projects already funded, at this link.  Thank you!

That’s all for now – thanks for reading!

With best wishes,

Ellie

 

You received this email because you signed up to Ellie’s mailing list on her website, took a campaign action, or gave us your address at an event. Published and promoted by Helen Heathfield on behalf of Ellie Chowns (Green Party), c/o 9 The Buttercross, Leominster, HR6 8BN.

If you are forwarding this email to a friend, then please mention they can sign up themselves for Ellie's updates here.

Late March 2025

Hello everyone

Last time I wrote was on Thursday 13th March, on the train home.  So here’s the update since then!

On 14th & 15th March I was busy in the constituency.  I held my constituency surgery in Withington Village Hall, a new venue for me – part of my efforts to make my surgeries accessible to as many people as possible by holding them all over North Herefordshire.

After that I went to meet with the new editor of the Hereford Times, Alicia Kelly, to hear from her about her plans for the paper.  The HT is a local institution!  Alicia is keen to ensure the paper represents content from all over the county, and reflects local people’s interests and priorities – so if you know of a good local story, don’t hesitate to let her know!

Above: Ellie and members of the Home-Start team.

I then went to visit Home-Start, a local charity that supports families with young children.  I actually used to volunteer with Home-Start myself many years ago, so it was great to catch up with what they are doing now!

Next was a meeting with the Creative Health team and the Our Place project in Hereford city centre – not technically in the constituency, but it serves people from all over Herefordshire. We had an excellent discussion of how the arts and creativity can help address both physical and mental health problems – for example, singing is good for asthma!  And it was great to see the fantastic exhibition in their unit on Gomond Street.

From there I went to visit Herefordshire Local Optometric Committee members to discuss how eye health problems are treated in the county – very interesting.

And in the evening I was hosted in Colwall village hall by Herefordshire for Europe and Malvern for Europe for a ‘fireside chat’ / Q&A event.  With an audience of well over a hundred people the questions roamed far and wide!

On the Saturday (after a bit of work on my inbox) I visited the site of the forthcoming new Leominster Health Hub, and met with two of the doctors involved and the site owner Steven Dale.  It’s great to see the building taking shape and it’ll be fantastic for Leominster to have this new facility!

Next I went to visit Eastnor Castle to learn more about the various businesses associated with the castle, and to have a look at the restored water mill and see flour being ground.  It’s quite something!

Above: Ellie with James and Lucy Hervey-Bathurst at Eastnor Castle.

On the Saturday evening I took the train back up to London because early on Sunday I was setting off on a much longer train journey – to the Netherlands, for an Environmental Audit Committee study visit.

Two of the current EAC inquiries are focusing on flood resilience, and on the environmental impact of new housing.  The committee thought there could be some lessons to learn from the Netherlands in respect of both these areas, hence this visit. We packed a lot into the three days: meetings with water department officials and embassy staff and the British Ambassador; visits to a water management applied research institute and an experimental university site where building innovations are tested; a walking tour of Rotterdam’s water management infrastructure; site visits to a huge flood protection ‘water gate’ and two different beach replenishment projects and a ‘room for the river’ site where they’ve allowed the river to flood naturally in order to protect built-up areas elsewhere… it was all absolutely fascinating.  Admittedly, there’s a limit to the transferability of some of the tech – given that the topography of the UK is, shall we say, quite different! But one of the most fascinating lessons was the way that the Dutch approach seems to be to start with the question ‘what do we need to do to protect our infrastructure’ and then to get on with a prioritised, agreed, long term plan plus the funding to do it.  It’s an outcome-focused approach, whereas too often the UK approach seems to be input-focused, i.e. ‘what’s the smallest amount of money we can get away with putting into activity X until the next election’…  Very frustrating!  So I would very much like to see the UK adopt a more ‘Dutch’, outcome-focused approach to flood resilience investment and infrastructure planning.  I am sure our committee will be including something along these lines in our recommendations!

I returned to the UK (again by train) on the Wednesday evening, which meant I had sadly missed three days of business in the Commons.  Frustratingly, this included the Report Stage of the Children’s Wellbeing and Schools Bill, on which I had done so much work in Committee.  I was very sad not to have been able to participate in the final debate – or vote.  However, my colleagues Carla and Adrian stepped in to ensure that Green voices were heard in the debate and our concerns raised again.  Adrian also managed to get a vote called on our amendment on expanding access to free school meals!  Sadly, the government opposed it.

On Thursday I took part in a meeting of the UK Trade and Business Commission, taking evidence from three experts on the future of the UK-EU relationship, including the prospects for the upcoming May Summit.

Back in the House of Commons, I was in the Chamber for David Lammy’s statement on the renewed Israeli air strikes on Gaza, and questioned him on whether UK-made arms or components had been used in those strikes.  There is much more that the UK can and should do to show our opposition to the Israeli government’s war crimes in Gaza.

Left: Ellie at the statement on Gaza on 20th March.  Click on the photo to see the video of her question.

I then met with the Alzheimer’s Society to learn more about their work and their priorities for the upcoming Commission on Social Care.   Following my question to the Leader of the House last week, I had been assured of a government response – but I still have not heard anything about the rescheduling of the cross-party talks on social care, and it’s now a month since they were cancelled. This really is not OK and I will be chasing Wes Streeting yet again this week about this.

After further meetings with colleagues on social care, I took the train home.

Friday back in the constituency was the usual wonderful mix!

I started the day with a meeting with the council chief executive, Paul Walker – a regular opportunity to ask him about outstanding issues that constituents have raised with me, and to discuss key issues in the county.

Next was a delightful visit to Staunton on Wye primary school, where I had a great time looking around the school, meeting all the different classes, admiring their artwork and joining in with their play, and answering some excellent questions!  I was struck that, when I asked them in assembly about what issues they wanted me to work on as their MP, many of them talked about environmental and animal welfare issues.  They were well-informed and a delight to meet!

My surgery was held in Whitney on Wye, right at the edge of the constituency – again, part of my effort to reach out widely!  However, despite substantial efforts to publicise the surgery, only two people registered, so there’s clearly lots more demand elsewhere.

After a couple of hours back in the office in Leominster for a team meeting, I headed to Hereford Quarries for my last visit of the day.  I love a ‘factory tour’ – it’s always so interesting to see how things work behind the scenes – and this was no exception.  The site processes both new and recycled material, and it was fascinating to see how they wash and sort it – and how so much, including the water, is recycled.

Above: Ellie visiting Lugg Bridge Quarry, Hereford Quarries.

On Saturday I spent the day out and about in Worcestershire and Warwickshire, supporting Green Party local election candidates – fantastic to meet so many enthusiastic and energised local campaigners doing brilliant jobs getting more Greens elected!

And on Saturday evening I went to Hereford Cathedral for a wonderful Hereford Choral Society performance of Vivaldi’s Gloria and two other pieces.  I used to sing in the choir (and my dad still does) so it’s always especially lovely to go to their concerts!

As it happens, I have some music to look forward to in Parliament this week – but I’ll save that story for the next update!

Also coming up this week – today in fact – is the Planning and Infrastructure Bill. A significant piece of legislation, and I have requested to speak in the debate today.  So I’m going to stop here, and go and work on that now!

As always, thank you for reading and for your support!  And do encourage your friends to subscribe if they’d like to keep in touch with my work.

With best wishes,

Ellie

You received this email because you signed up to Ellie’s mailing list on her website, took a campaign action, or gave us your address at an event. Published and promoted by Helen Heathfield on behalf of Ellie Chowns (Green Party), c/o 9 The Buttercross, Leominster, HR6 8BN.

If you are forwarding this email to a friend, then please mention they can sign up themselves for Ellie's updates here.

Mid March 2025

Hello all

I’m writing this on the train back to Herefordshire late on Thursday night after another busy week in Westminster, so I’ve got two weeks of Parliamentary activity to update you on…

Thinking back to 10 days ago, the big focus was on international affairs.  I spoke in the House on Monday responding to the government’s Ukraine statement, and had my first Urgent Question accepted by the Speaker on Tuesday, on Gaza. An Urgent Question is when a backbencher can call a Minister to the House to speak about an urgent issue; MPs submit questions first thing and the Speaker decides whether to grant any of them. Some days we have none, sometimes one or two, occasionally three.

Also on Tuesday I met with the organisation Patriotic Millionaires (wealthy people who support a wealth tax), was briefed by an LSE professor on climate risks, met with a constituent who is in prison, attended multiple drop-ins, took part in a meeting on rebuilding the NHS, and attended an International Women’s Day reception with inspiring young women from organisations like Girl Guiding and the WI.

The next day, women from all parties gathered in the Chamber for a photo celebrating the large number of women MPs – more than ever before.  We’re still not a 50/50 Parliament though, and there’s still a long way to go until true equality!

I had a very positive meeting with the Secretary of State for Education regarding the campaign for a new Natural History GCSE, which is something Caroline Lucas had been spearheading before me.

I attended PMQs, then took part in the Estimates Day debate on the Foreign, Commonweath and Development Office, where I challenged the government’s shockingly callous, counterproductive and short-sighted decision to slash aid.

On Thursday I met with my Monmouth MP colleague Catherine Fookes to discuss our shared interest in work to clean up the River Wye, then attended Business Questions where I asked the Leader of the House why I’d still had no contact from the Health Secretary following the last-minute cancellation of the Cross-Party Commission on Social Care launch meeting two weeks earlier.  

I stayed in the Chamber much of the day, since we then had a statement on North Sea oil (I challenged the government not to issue consents for oil and gas fields that have not yet started production); then a very moving debate for International Women’s Day, including Jess Phillips reading out the names of all the women who had been killed by men in the previous year.

The Parliamentary week ended with a debate on reforming political finance, where I called for limits to donations, tighter restrictions on foreign donations, and more powers for the Electoral Commission to stop breaches.

The following day, Friday 7th March, was actually my 50th birthday, so I hope you all don’t mind that I took the weekend off to spend time with family and friends!  I had a wonderful birthday – an early morning swim in Ledbury, a long walk on the Malverns, tea and cake with my neighbours, a Thai takeaway with my sons and my parents, and then a festive evening at my local open mic night!  On the Saturday I briefly popped into the Herefordshire Women’s Equality Group ‘women at work’ event at NMITE in Hereford, then headed up to Snowdonia to stay overnight in a youth hostel, go on the fastest zip wire in the world with my two sons, and climb Tryfan!  We were blessed with absolutely glorious weather all weekend, and it was a wonderful break – even if my legs were a bit sore the next day!😊

Back to Westminster on Monday 10th and more international affairs, with a statement on the deeply concerning violence in Syria.  On Tuesday my focus shifted back to domestic issues: I ‘guested’ on the Environment, Food and Rural Affairs Select Committee’s evidence session with Welsh Water; responded to the government’s statement on the North Sea tanker collision, and took part in an NSPCC event.  In the evening I was really honoured and delighted to be named one of the ‘One Hundred Women of Westminster’ feted by the House Magazine, alongside a number of other MPs, journalists, and other Westminster movers and shakers.  On Tuesday and Wednesday we were also voting on the Employment Rights Bill.

Late on Tuesday night I started receiving emails from farmers in my constituency, horrified that the government had just cancelled the Sustainable Farming Incentive scheme with no notice at all – despite having previously said they would give 6 weeks notice if the scheme had to close.  So I ‘bobbed’ throughout PMQs on Wednesday, hoping to be able to ask a question on this, but wasn’t selected.  However, directly afterwards we had a ministerial statement on the issue, so I was able to challenge the farming minister Daniel Zeichner directly.  I had a second pop on Thursday, during the Future of Farming debate; and I was on BBC Hereford and Worcester about the issue on Thursday morning.  It’s simply shocking for the government to treat farmers this way.

Also on Thursday I was able to ask the Secretary of State for Business and Trade about the devastating impact of the planning moratorium on the construction sector in North Herefordshire. I’m delighted that he agreed to meet with me and representatives of the local industry to discuss how to solve the issue. (Government announced £1 million on Tuesday for research to tackle pollution in the Wye, which is a step in the right direction, but as I have said for many, many months, we need far more support).

I also raised – again! – the issue of the delayed Social Care Commission talks.  I have been promised a response and I won’t stop chasing the government until they get going on this issue – it’s just so important.

Somewhat unexpectedly I had a call from ITV today asking if I could come on their politics panel discussion programme at the last minute – so of course I said yes!  The topics were Brexit and benefits cuts.  I’m shocked – but no longer surprised – that Labour are apparently planning yet again to balance the books on the backs of the poorest by cutting disability benefits; I’ve had large numbers of emails from worried constituents this week.  Government needs to help people to work if they can, and support them if they can’t.  But what they are doing is creating huge anxiety for anyone who is sick or disabled.  It’s frankly shocking – especially when (as I have said before) there is another way: Rachel Reeves could choose to increase taxes on wealth instead.

I’m back in the constituency for the next couple of days – holding a surgery for constituents in Withington, meeting the new editor of the Hereford Times, meeting local organisations Home-Start (who I used to volunteer with!), Creative Health / Our Place, and Ophthalmology Hereford; speaking at an event in Colwall with Herefordshire for Europe; visiting the site of the new North Herefordshire Health Hub in Leominster, and visiting Eastnor Castle to discuss the tourist economy.

I’m then back up to London on Saturday night, because next week is rather unusual for me – I’m taking the train to the Netherlands along with many of my colleagues from the Environmental Audit Select Committee for a study visit as part of our inquiry into Flood Resilience.  The Netherlands obviously has a lot of valuable experience in this area!  I’m looking forward to what should be a fascinating visit – but I confess I am sad to be away from Westminster too, especially as next Monday and Tuesday are the Report Stage of the Children’s Wellbeing and Schools Bill on which I did so much work in Committee.  My colleagues Carla and Adrian are kindly stepping in to cover the Report Stage debates, but unfortunately the study visit means I won’t be able to vote on the Bill.  This is yet another reason why electronic voting would be so much better – MPs could still vote if they were unavoidably away from the House on official business.

Looking forward a bit further, the Planning and Infrastructure Bill had its (very brief) First Reading this week, and will have its (much longer) Second Reading debate on Monday 24th March.  This is an absolutely crucial piece of legislation, and I will be taking a very close interest in it and leading on it for the Green MPs; I’m very much hoping to get a place on the Bill Committee for this one.

All in all, there’s plenty to get my teeth into! 😊

As always, do let me know what issues are important to you; do please follow me on my social media channels, and please encourage your friends and neighbours to do the same and to subscribe to this newsletter.

And if you have suggestions as to how I can improve these updates, please let me know!

With very best wishes,

Ellie

 

You received this email because you signed up to Ellie’s mailing list on her website, took a campaign action, or gave us your address at an event. Published and promoted by Helen Heathfield on behalf of Ellie Chowns (Green Party), c/o 9 The Buttercross, Leominster, HR6 8BN.

If you are forwarding this email to a friend, then please mention they can sign up themselves for Ellie's updates here.

Early March 2025

You know the saying ‘a week is a long time in politics’? Well, more or less every week feels like that – but last week even more so than most.

In Westminster

The Parliamentary week was dominated by questions of defence and international development, as Starmer prepared to meet Trump. On Tuesday the Prime Minister announced that he was slashing the international aid budget in order to ramp up investment in defence. While I recognise the importance of properly funding our defence budget, I utterly disagree with doing so by cutting aid. I challenged the PM on this directly in the Chamber, pointing out that he could find the money elsewhere (namely, by increasing taxes on the very wealthiest) and that slashing aid to the poorest and most fragile countries is unbelievably shortsighted and counterproductive. It will make the world less secure in the long run, and in the short term it opens up strategic space globally for Russia and China to increase their influence. Frankly, I’m furious. Essentially, our Prime Minister has thrown the world’s poorest under the bus on the altar of appeasing Trump. It’s shameful. (And yes, I know I’m mixing metaphors).

And while it was all smiles between Starmer and Trump on Thursday, the wheels came off by Friday. My goodness me. I could hardly believe my ears listening to the broadcast from the White House on Friday evening. Trump and Vance’s outrageous public bullying and attempted humiliation of Zelensky was extraordinary.

It was good to see a united front from European leaders on Sunday. Let’s see what the coming week brings.

 

On the domestic policy front, I spent time last week preparing for the first meeting of the Cross-Party Commission on Social Care, a cross-party initiative where MPs, peers, and experts come together to tackle the systemic issues affecting our care system and lay the groundwork for a national care service. Yet sadly this meeting was postponed, apparently because one of the opposition parties (I can guess which one) couldn’t make it. The delay is deeply frustrating, given the urgency of tackling this huge stain on our country’s conscience.

On the more positive side, I attended an awards ceremony in Parliament for the National Association of Local Councils, in which Will Morris from Fownhope Parish Council was a finalist for ‘Young Councillor of the Year’. It was great to see his work as a parish footpaths officer recognised – as well

as the hard work of all those who give up their time to serve their communities as councillors across the country.

 

I also spoke at the Children’s Food Summit on how we can build political consensus on children’s healthy food. I spoke in a Westminster Hall debate on improving access to banking in rural areas – a key issue affecting many in North Herefordshire. And I took part in a select committee evidence session with leaders of the Office for Environmental Protection.

Of course, there’s also lots of work that doesn’t go on social media feeds. Lots of my time is spent responding to emails from constituents, for example. And there are also lots of background meetings. For instance, last week I had a briefing about the ‘restoration and renewal’ project – a huge project needed to tackle building safety problems in the Palace of Westminster. And I also spent a fascinating couple of hours with senior lawyers, academics, and military leaders discussing the Chagos Islands deal.

I also occasionally get to go off site – and on Thursday I joined colleagues from the Environmental Audit Select Committee for a visit to the National Oceanography Centre (NOC) in Southampton. It was an absolutely fascinating day – so informative. The huge amount of expertise at NOC is deeply impressive – and crucial to helping us understand and address the climate crisis.

This couldn’t be more topical, given that the independent Committee on Climate Change (CCC) published its Seventh Carbon Budget (7CB) last Wednesday. The 7CB sets out what we need to do to meet our legally binding climate change targets. It shows that getting to net zero is both doable and extraordinarily good value. The cost of the investment needed is about 0.2% of GDP, the same amount the PM boosted defence by last week.

When we recognise a crisis, we find the resources to deal with it. We did it with Covid. We did it with WW2. We need to tackle the climate crisis with the same seriousness of purpose and the same commitment. What’s so extraordinary about our collective failure to do so is that the prize is so clear. Warm homes, good jobs, clean air, cheaper public transport, and a liveable future for our children. What on earth is stopping us?

In the constituency

 

On Friday morning I held my usual weekly surgery, this time in Bromyard, then headed off to Hereford to visit the wonderful National Star College, which provides educational opportunities for young people age 18-25 with disabilities. It was an absolute joy to visit the college – I was shown round by the students themselves, and invited to play under a parachute in the very first room I entered! I had a peek at their exciting new extension which will enable them to provide even better facilities, and heard about their ‘reverse jobs fair’ where students are able to showcase their skills and attributes to potential employers and work experience placements.

I then visited Eziserve, a local business with a long history of innovating in packaging design, to hear about how their ‘no-touch’ design for dispensing chemicals has hugely reduced spillages and contamination.

Later that afternoon, I met with the co-chairs of Farm Herefordshire to discuss current issues in farming and work to tackle river pollution.

Then, on Friday evening, I had the wonderful privilege of being one of the three judges for ‘Ledbury’s Got Talent’ at John Masefield High School – an opportunity to channel my inner Shirley Ballas! Honestly, I was blown away by the talent on show! The fantastic singing, dancing and musicianship throughout the night made it really hard to choose winners. In the end, we gave first prize to the JMHS rock band, who performed a song they’d written themselves with huge gusto and talent. What a brilliant evening!

The musical theme continued the following day as I took the Saturday morning off to join Hereford Choral Society’s ‘Sing for Fun’ workshop, singing Mozart’s Requiem – wonderful.

And on Saturday afternoon I met with local and regional National Trust staff at Berrington Hall to hear about their work regenerating the gardens at the Hall and the Trust’s new strategy, which puts restoring nature and equalising access front and centre. The sun shone brightly and the conversations gave me hope!

In the media

 

I’ve been on the radio a bit this week. On Wednesday, Evan Davis and the Radio 4 PM programme returned to North Herefordshire to follow up on their visit during the General Election campaign. I joined the programme to discuss how a thriving economy and thriving environment can and must work hand in hand.

On Saturday I was on the Week in Westminster on Radio 4, discussing the government’s cuts to the aid budget and the need for a much more strategic approach to defence and security.

No doubt these topics will continue to be at the top of the agenda in the coming week.

I’ve also had op-eds published in Politics.co.uk and The House Magazine on defence spending and my proposed equal protection from harm amendment, respectively.

As always, please follow my social media channels to keep up to date with what I’m doing on a daily basis.

And do please share these updates with any of your friends and neighbours who you think might be interested – and encourage them to subscribe via this link: https://elliechowns.org.uk/updates/

With best wishes,

Ellie

You received this email because you signed up to Ellie’s mailing list on her website, took a campaign action, or gave us your address at an event. Published and promoted by Helen Heathfield on behalf of Ellie Chowns (Green Party), c/o 9 The Buttercross, Leominster, HR6 8BN.

If you are forwarding this email to a friend, then please mention they can sign up themselves for Ellie's updates here.

Late February 2025

Hello everyone.  I’m back in Westminster again this week, after spending last week in the constituency, since Parliament was in recess.

In the constituency

It was really lovely to be based in my constituency office all week; great to have a proper chance to discuss casework with each of my brilliant team.  I think I’ve mentioned before that I get over a thousand emails each week, and I couldn’t possibly deal with all the issues raised by constituents without the support of my amazing staff.  Together we’ve all been trying hard over the past few weeks to tackle the slight backlog of correspondence that had built up while I was getting staff recruited and systems sorted.  We’re getting there now! 😊

The week in North Herefordshire actually started on Friday 14th, which began with an online meeting with Ornua, the company that is sadly closing its Ledbury factory; I have been pressing them to do everything possible to assist staff whose jobs are at risk. 

I then I held a constituency surgery in Kington, where residents brought issues to me including pollution, housing, and benefits.  After a quick cuppa in the Border Bean café, I visited a farm and small business just outside the town.

In the afternoon I met with local representatives of RoadPeace, the national charity for road crash victims; it was very powerful to hear their testimony, and I’ll certainly be supporting their campaigning in Parliament. 

Last but not least that day, I visited the wonderful new NHS Community Diagnostic Centre that is under construction next to B&Q in Hereford, which will be opening later this year.  It’s a great facility which will make it easier and quicker for patients to get the diagnostic tests they need such as MRI scans. 

It’s a very environmentally-friendly building, designed by local architects and built by local builders; and it's also fully publicly owned, funded and run!  I’m delighted that my questioning of Wes Streeting back in January helped secure confirmation of the final chunk of funding for phase 1, and next I’ll be helping to press the government for funding for phase 2!

Last week I was also out and about in the community at various events including a business breakfast, a care leavers’ fair, the wonderfully creative Light Up Leominster, and the delicious Pencombe Young Farmers’ Club Big Breakfast!

There’s lots to report on from the week before the recess, too.

In Westminster

 

On Monday 10th I spoke in the Westminster Hall debate on Agricultural Property Relief and inheritance tax.  I also attended a meeting of the APPG on Farming, and the launch of a report by the UK Trade and Business Commission, of which I am a member.

On Tuesday 11th we had the last day of the Children’s Wellbeing and Schools Bill Committee, at which I proposed amendments on expanding eligibility for free school meals and on equal protection for children against assault. The government did not support these, but I’m going to continue working with campaigners and raising these issues at the Report Stage of the Bill. 

 

At DHSC Oral Questions that day I questioned Wes Streeting on elective care waiting lists and his attitude to private sector involvement in the NHS. 

Later on, I attended the Mandela +20 celebrations in Trafalgar Square, and then spoke at a Fostering Network event paying tribute to the amazing work of foster carers. 

On Wednesday 12th I spoke in a Westminster Hall Debate on fuel poverty, which is a huge issue in North Herefordshire.  I also attended the inaugural meeting of the APPG on Nature Recovery. I co-sponsored the Ten Minute Rule Bill on capping political donations, proposed by my Lib Dem colleague Manuela Perteghella.  My select committee, the Environmental Audit Committee, held another evidence session on Flood Resilience, taking evidence from witnesses including from the TCPA, CIWEM and NFU.  After that I attended a ‘Yellow Wellies’ drop in on farmers’ mental health, and then spoke in another debate on the ICJ judgements regarding the Occupied Palestinian Territories.

On Thursday 13th I took part in the Urgent Question session on Ukraine, and pressed the government to stand up to and speak out against Trump’s outrageous disinformation.  I’m very disappointed in the UK’s continuing failure to do so.  Later that same day I challenged the government’s deeply concerning decision to prevent ‘illegal’ refugees from applying for citizenship – a ridiculous and cruel policy, especially since there are virtually no safe and legal routes for refugees to come to the UK.  I also met that day with colleagues regarding my participation in the all-party Commission on Social Care, and with cross-party colleagues, NGOs and health professionals regarding the ongoing campaign for equal protection for children.

Coming up this week

OK, I think that brings you up to date!  My key focus in this coming week is preparing for the All Party Commission on Social Care; I’m meeting with Wes Streeting, Baroness Casey, and other party leaders and representatives on Wednesday, and with Professor Andrew Dilnot today.

There’s also loads more going on – but I’ll report back on that next week!
In the meantime, please do share these updates with any of your friends and neighbours who you think might be interested – and encourage them to subscribe too!  Here’s the link: https://elliechowns.org.uk/updates/

You received this email because you signed up to Ellie’s mailing list on her website, took a campaign action, or gave us your address at an event. Published and promoted by Helen Heathfield on behalf of Ellie Chowns (Green Party), c/o 9 The Buttercross, Leominster, HR6 8BN.

If you are forwarding this email to a friend, then please mention they can sign up themselves for Ellie's updates here.

Mid February 2025

Hello everyone – here goes with another quick update written from the train heading into London for another week at the Westminster coalface! 😊

A key part of my work over the last couple of weeks has been on the Children’s Wellbeing and Schools (CWS) Bill Committee.  Our last sitting day is 11th February.  We’ve been through about 80 amendments so far and now we’re going through the new clauses of which there are more than 70…  It’s been a fascinating process.  I’ve proposed amendments on a range of things including extending ‘Staying Put’ provision (so young people can stay with their foster families for longer) and introducing family group decision making before family reunification.  I’ve also supported, and verbally proposed to the committee, amendments tabled by other MPs, particularly on equal protection for children from assault, and on expanding eligibility for free school meals.  I’ve learned a lot about how the committee process works – including the fact that you don’t always ‘push your amendment to a vote’ because you might be playing a longer game i.e. trying to persuade the government to see the wisdom of your arguments so that they might perhaps decide to ‘adopt’ your amendment as their own at some future point… On both equal protection and free school meals, I very much hope this will be the case.

The Bill Committee has taken up a lot of my time in the last fortnight – and I was knocked flat by a sickness bug for a few days too, unfortunately – but there’s been lots else going on besides.  Two weeks ago my Select Committee (the Environmental Audit Committee) had Ed Miliband (Secretary of State for Energy Security and Net Zero) up in front of us for scrutiny.  It was just before the long-trailed announcement of government support for airport expansion – clearly a terrible decision – so I took the opportunity to grill Ed on the inconsistency of government policy on this.  You can read about the session here.
